text:

is connected to two long vertical frictionless rails. The bar and the rails have
negligible resistance. They are placed in a uniform magnetic field of strength B directed into the page as shown
above. The bottoms of the rails are connected by a resistor of resistance R. The bar is released from rest in the

(b) In the figure above, use appropriate symbols to indicate the direction of the magnetic field both above and
below the bottom bar due to its current I.

constants, as appropriate.
The bars are now used in an experiment, as shown in the figure below. The bottom bar is fixed in place. The top
bar is suspended from/springs (that are not shown), is free to move up and down, and has a pan attached to it for
adding small weights. The top bar is originally horizontal and balanced in the equilibrium position as shown. No

shown) and connected to a variable power supply. A small object is placed in the pan on the top bar, forcing it
down until it comes close to the bottom bar. The current is turned on and increased until it pushes the top bar
back to its original equilibrium position. The process is repeated several times with different objects of known
mass, and the current is measured with an ammeter each time. The resulting data are given in the table below. The figure shows two horizontal metal bars of equal length. The top one

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2015 Practice Exam
text:
You perform a laboratory experiment to determine the unknown charge on a small conducting ball of mass m
using the experimental setup shown in the diagram above. A variable power supply applies a DC voltage to two
large parallel plates separated by a distance d, creating a uniform electric field. The charged ball hangs between
the plates on an insulated thread of length L and is displaced from its lowest point, coming to equilibrium at an
angle q with the vertical. During the experiment, you measure the angle q when the voltage indicated on the
power supply is V .
(a) On the dot below that represents the conducting ball, draw and label the forces (not components) that act on
the conducting ball. Each force must be represented by a distinct arrow starting on, and pointing away from,
the dot.
(b) Derive an expression that would allow you to calculate the magnitude of the unknown charge on the ball
given q , V , m, d, L, and fundamental constants, as appropriate. If you need to draw anything other than
what you have shown in part (a) to assist in your solution, use the space below. Do NOT add anything to the
figure in part (a).
(c) One way to determine a more accurate value for the magnitude of the charge on the conducting ball in the
experiment is to perform multiple trials.
i. What quantity would you vary in this experiment to obtain different values of the angle q ?
ii. What quantities would you plot on a graph to obtain a linear relationship that can be used to determine
the magnitude of the charge on the conducting ball?
(d) Describe one difficulty in precisely and accurately determining the angle q with a protractor, and describe a
method to overcome the difficulty.
(e) If the voltage is high enough, the ball touches one of the plates. Describe what happens from the time it
touches until you turn off the voltage.

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2016 Practice Exam
text:
A rod of length $L$ lies along the $x$-axis with its left end at the origin, as shown in the figure. The rod has a nonuniform linear charge density $\lambda=\alpha x$, where $\alpha$ is a positive constant.

(a) Determine the units of the constant $\alpha$.

(b) Derive an expression for the total charge on the rod. Express your answer in terms of $\alpha$, $L$, and physical constants, as appropriate.

The rod is replaced with one of the same length but with a uniform positive linear charge density $\lambda_0$.

(c) Show that the electric field at point $A$, which is a distance $b$ to the left of the rod, has a magnitude of $E=\frac{\lambda_0 L}{4\pi\epsilon_0 b(b+L)}$.

(d) A proton is placed at point $A$, at position $(-b,0)$, near the positively charged rod and released from rest.

(i) Sketch a graph of the horizontal component of the acceleration of the proton $a_x$ as a function of position $x$ for the region in which the proton travels after it is released. Sketch any asymptote, but do not indicate its value.

(ii) Sketch a graph of the horizontal component of the velocity of the proton $v_x$ as a function of position $x$ for the region in which the proton travels after it is released. Sketch any asymptote, but do not indicate its value.

(iii) Instead of a proton, an alpha particle with twice the charge and four times the mass of a proton is now placed at point $A$. Is the magnitude of the initial acceleration of the alpha particle greater than, less than, or equal to the magnitude of the initial acceleration of the proton from part (d)(i)?

(e) Using the equation from part (c), show that the magnitude of the electric field at a point on the $x$-axis very far away from the rod is approximately equal to the electric field due to a point charge of magnitude $Q=\lambda_0 L$ located at the origin.

points.description: Solve $\lambda=\alpha x$ for $\alpha$ and give units $\mathrm{C/m^2}$.
Integrate the charge density over the rod with correct limits to obtain $Q=\frac12\alpha L^2$.
Use Coulomb's law with $dq=\lambda_0\,dr$ and integrate from the near end to the far end to derive $E=\frac{\lambda_0 L}{4\pi\epsilon_0 b(b+L)}$.
Sketch $a_x$ beginning at $x=-b$, directed in the negative $x$ direction, approaching zero with the horizontal axis as an asymptote.
Sketch $v_x$ beginning at $x=-b$, remaining below the horizontal axis, concave up, and approaching a nonzero horizontal asymptote.
Select less than, because the alpha particle has twice the charge but four times the mass.
Use $b\gg L$ so $b(b+L)\approx b^2$ and substitute $Q=\lambda_0L$ to show $E\approx \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\frac{Q}{b^2}$.

notes: Structured from the official 2016 PDF; focused diagram/rubric images are supplemental.

evidence: Classified from FRQ prompt and official scoring guideline using Source Pack units U8-U13.

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2016 Practice Exam
text:
A circuit consists of a variable DC power supply, an ammeter, a switch, and a resistor of unknown resistance $R_0$ connected in series with a test space into which different circuit elements can be placed. To determine $R_0$, a wire of negligible resistance is placed in the test space. The power supply is set at several potential differences, and the current is measured.

| $\epsilon$ (V) | 2.00 | 4.00 | 6.00 | 8.00 |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Current (mA) | 0.146 | 0.310 | 0.433 | 0.577 |

(a) Indicate which quantities should be graphed to yield a straight line whose slope can be used to calculate $R_0$. Include horizontal and vertical axes.

(b) Plot the straight-line data points on the graph. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units, if appropriate. Draw a straight line that best represents the data.

(c) Using your straight line, calculate an experimental value for $R_0$.

(d) The power supply is set to $4.00\ \mathrm{V}$ and an uncharged air-filled capacitor is placed in the test space. The switch is closed at $t=0$, and the capacitor is allowed to fully charge.

(i) On the axes shown, sketch the current $I$ through the ammeter as a function of time $t$. Explicitly label intercepts, asymptotes, maxima, or minima with numerical values or algebraic expressions, as appropriate.

(ii) The time constant is $0.045\ \mathrm{s}$ and the capacitor is charged to $4\ \mathrm{V}$. Calculate the energy stored in the capacitor.

(iii) The graph shows the charge $Q$ on the capacitor as a function of time $t$. The capacitor is discharged, a dielectric with dielectric constant $\kappa=2$ is inserted, and the same charging procedure is repeated. On the graph, sketch the curve showing $Q$ for the dielectric-filled capacitor as a function of $t$.

points.description: Choose graph quantities such as current on one axis and potential difference $\epsilon$ on the other; axes may be reversed.
Label axes with variables and units, use an appropriate scale, correctly plot the data, and draw a reasonable best-fit straight line.
Calculate the slope from the best-fit line, relate $\epsilon=IR$ so $R$ equals the slope, and obtain about $13.95\ \mathrm{k\Omega}$ (linear regression about $14.08\ \mathrm{k\Omega}$).
Sketch a concave-up current curve in the first quadrant that approaches the horizontal axis and correctly labels the initial current.
Use $\tau=RC$ to find $C\approx3.23\times10^{-6}\ \mathrm{F}$ and use $U=\frac12CV^2$ to find $U\approx2.58\times10^{-5}\ \mathrm{J}$.
Sketch a concave-down charge curve starting at the origin, above the original curve, asymptoting later and at twice the original final charge.

notes: Structured from the official 2016 PDF; focused diagram/rubric images are supplemental.

evidence: Classified from FRQ prompt and official scoring guideline using Source Pack units U8-U13.

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2016 Practice Exam
text:
An ideal battery is connected to three resistors, an ideal inductor, and two switches, as shown in the circuit. Both switches are initially open. Switch $S_2$ is then closed.

(a) Calculate the voltage across resistor $R_2$.

Switch $S_1$ is then closed.

(b) Calculate the magnitude of the rate of change of the current through the inductor immediately after switch $S_1$ is closed.

(c) At time $t$ after switch $S_1$ is closed, the instantaneous current through resistor $R_2$ is measured to be $0.020\ \mathrm{A}$.

(i) Calculate the current through resistor $R_3$ at time $t$.

(ii) Calculate the energy stored in the inductor at time $t$.

After a long time, the currents reach a steady value. Switch $S_2$ is then opened.

(d) Calculate the current through the inductor immediately after switch $S_2$ is opened.

(e) Write, but do not solve, a differential equation that can be used to determine the current through resistor $R_2$ at time $t$ after switch $S_2$ is opened.

(f) Using the differential equation from part (e), derive an expression for the current through resistor $R_2$ as a function of time $t$ after switch $S_2$ is opened.

points.description: Use Ohm's law to find the current through the resistors and then the voltage across $R_2$, obtaining $10\ \mathrm{V}$.
Use the inductor relation $\epsilon_L=-L\,di/dt$ and the voltage across $R_2$ to find $|di/dt|\approx3.3\ \mathrm{A/s}$.
Use Ohm's law to find the voltage across $R_2$ and then the current through $R_3$, obtaining about $0.18\ \mathrm{A}$.
Use $U=\frac12Li^2$ with the inductor current consistent with part (c)(i), obtaining about $0.0384\ \mathrm{J}$.
Use Ohm's law for the long-time steady-state circuit to determine the inductor current, obtaining $0.20\ \mathrm{A}$.
Use the loop rule to relate inductor emf to the voltage drop across $R_2$ and write a correct differential equation such as $-L\,di/dt=iR_2$.
Separate variables, integrate with correct limits or constant of integration, and derive $i(t)=(0.20\ \mathrm{A})e^{-33.3t}$.

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2017 Practice Exam
text:
A student is to design a circuit using a battery
ε with negligible internal resistance, two uncharged capacitors
C1 and C2 , a resistor R, and a switch S. The circuit should be set up so that when the switch is in one position,
the battery will only charge capacitor C1, and when in the second position, capacitor C1 will discharge through
capacitor C2 and resistor R.
(a) Using the components shown below, draw a circuit diagram that represents a single circuit that will satisfy
the criteria outlined above.
The switch S is initially in position to fully charge capacitor C1. At time t = 0, the switch is moved to the second
position. The charge as a function of time for both capacitors after the switch is moved is plotted on the graph
below.
(b) Label each box to indicate which graph corresponds to C1 and C2 .
R = 1000 W , C1 = 2.0 µF, C2 = 6.0 µF , e = 12 V
The components of the circuit have the following values:
.
(c) Calculate the following for a long time after the switch has been moved to the second position.
i. The charges q1 and q2 stored on capacitors C1 and C2 , respectively
ii. The potential differences V1 and V2 across the capacitors C1 and C2 , respectively
(d) Calculate the total energy dissipated in resistor R during the time capacitor C1 discharges.
(e)
i. Write, but do NOT solve, a differential equation that could be used to determine the time constant
associated with the discharge of C1 .
ii. Calculate the time constant associated with the discharge of C1.

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2017 Practice Exam
text:
A square loop of conducting wire with resistance R and with sides of length s is located in the xy-plane.
The loop is being pushed at constant speed v by an external force of magnitude Fext in the +x-direction through
a region with a magnetic field that is directed perpendicular to the xy-plane into the page, as shown in the figure
above. The magnitude of the magnetic field as a function of position x is given by the equation
( ) = cx
B x
+ B0 ,
where x is in meters, c is a positive constant in T m , and B0 is a positive constant in teslas. The effects of
gravity are negligible.
(a) Derive an expression for the magnetic flux through the loop when the left side of the loop is at
position x = x0 . Express your answer in terms of s, c, B0, x0 , and physical constants, as appropriate.
(b) Derive an expression for the magnitude of the induced current in the loop, if any. Express your answer in
terms of R, s, v, c, B0, x, and physical constants, as appropriate.
(c) Is the induced current in the loop clockwise, counterclockwise, or zero?
Clockwise Counterclockwise Zero ____ ____ ____
Justify your answer.
(d) When the left side of the loop is at position x = x0 and moving with constant speed v, what is the direction
of the net magnetic force, if any, on the loop?
Toward the top of the page
Out of the page
Left ____ ____ ____
____ Toward the bottom of the page
Into the page
Right
Undefined, because the net magnetic force on the loop is zero.
Justify your answer.
____ ____ ____
(e) Derive an expression for the magnitude of the external force Fext exerted on the loop to keep it moving at a
constant speed when the loop is at position x = x0. Express your answer in terms of R, s, v, c, B0, x0 , and
physical constants, as appropriate.
(f) Rank, with 1 being the largest, the magnitude of the magnetic force on the four sides of the loop. If two sides
have the same magnetic force, give them the same numerical ranking.
Left Right Top Bottom ____ _____ _____ ____

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2018 Practice Exam
text:
A circuit is created with an ideal battery and five resistors with the values shown in the figure above.
An open switch is shown that can connect points X and Y. A voltmeter V and an ammeter A sub 1 are also
shown in the figure.
(a) The switch is in the open position.
1 Calculate the current measured by the ammeter A sub 1.
2
Calculate the potential difference measured by the voltmeter V.
(b) The switch is now moved to the closed position.
1 Calculate the current measured by the ammeter A sub 1.
____ ____ ____
Question 1 continues on the next page.
2
Calculate the potential difference measured by the voltmeter V.
A student wants to determine if there is a current in the closed switch. The switch is now replaced with
ammeter A sub 2 between points X and Y. The ammeter acts as a closed switch and can measure the current,
if any, between the two points.
The figure presents a circuit identical to the previous circuit, except that switch S is replaced by an ammeter A 2, and voltmeter V is removed. The left side has a battery labeled epsilon equals 20 volts. Moving up the left side and across the top, the next element is a resistor labeled R 1 equals 2 ohms. To the right of the resistor, the circuit splits into two vertical branches. Moving downward, the first vertical branch contains a resistor labeled R 2 equals 6 ohms, a point labeled X, and a resistor labeled R 4 equals 12 ohms. Moving downward, the second vertical branch contains a resistor labeled R 3 equals 12 ohms, a point labeled Y, and a resistor labeled R 5 equals 6 ohms. Points X and Y are directly across from each other and are connected by an ammeter labeled A 2. Continuing clockwise around the circuit, after the two vertical branches join together, an ammeter labeled A 1 is indicated on the bottom/side of the circuit.
(c)
1 Calculate the current measured by ammeter A sub 2 .
2
Indicate the direction of the current, if any, through the ammeter A sub 2 .
Left Right
Undefined, because the current is zero
____ ____
The figure presents a circuit identical to the first circuit, except that a 12 microfarad capacitor is in series with switch S between points X and Y, and voltmeter V is removed. The left side has a battery labeled epsilon equals 20 volts. Moving up the left side and across the top, the next element is a resistor labeled R 1 equals 2 ohms. To the right of the resistor, the circuit splits into two vertical branches. Moving downward, the first vertical branch contains a resistor labeled R 2 equals 6 ohms, a point labeled X, and a resistor labeled R 4 equals 12 ohms. Moving downward, the second vertical branch contains a resistor labeled R 3 equals 12 ohms, a point labeled Y, and a resistor labeled R 5 equals 6 ohms. Points X and Y are directly across from each other and are connected by an open switch in series with a capacitor labeled C equals 12 microfarads. Continuting clockwise around the circuit, after the two vertical branches join together, an ammeter labeled A 1 is indicated on the bottom/side of the circuit.
(d) The ammeter A sub 2 is replaced with switch S and capacitor
C
equals ,
12 microfarads , as shown in the figure above. Switch
S is closed.
1 Calculate the charge stored on the positive plate of capacitor C a long time after switch S is closed.
2
Which point, X or Y, is at a higher electric potential?
X Y
Justify your answer.

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2018 Practice Exam
text:
A coaxial cable is composed of a central conducting wire surrounded by a cylindrical insulator, and around
that is a conducting cylindrical shell with an insulating cover. A power supply of e m fepsilon is used at one end
to connect the two conducting regions, and a resistive load of resistance R is connected at the other end of
the cable to complete the circuit, as shown in the schematic diagram and circuit figure above.
Each of the following two figures present a cross section of the preceding coaxial cable from the perspective of the battery end. The central conducting wire is shown as a circle, enclosed in a larger circle, which is the cylindrical insulator. In each figure, a point P inside the cylindrical insulator is up and to the right and a distance b from the center of the cable. This insulator is enclosed in a larger circle, which is the cylindrical conducting shell, which is also enclosed in the insulating cover. In figure 1, a point Q is indicated outside of the coaxial cable entirely to the left of the center of the cable. In figure 2, there are no additional points indicated.
A cross section of the cable from the perspective of the power supply end is depicted above as if looking into the
left end of the cable. Point P is inside the cylindrical insulator a distance b from the center of the cable. Point Q
is outside the cable.
(a)
1 On figure 1 shown above left, draw an Amperian loop that could be used to determine the magnitude
of the magnetic field at point P in the diagram. Clearly mark this loop as “Loop 1.”
2
On figure 1, draw an Amperian loop that could be used to determine the magnitude of the magnetic
field at point Q in the diagram. Clearly mark this loop as “Loop 2.”
3
On figure 2 shown above right, draw an arrow to show the direction of the magnetic field at point P.
The arrow should start on and point away from the point in the direction of the magnetic field.
Question 2 continues on the next page.
(b) Using Ampere’s law, derive an expression for the magnitude of the magnetic field at point P. Express your
answer in terms of epsilon , R, b, and physical constants, as appropriate.
(c) Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field at point Q.
Justify your answer.
Some physics students conduct an experiment with a coaxial cable in which they connect the cable to a
variable power supply and measure the resulting magnetic field strength at point P inside the cylindrical
insulator using a magnetic field sensor. Point P is 0.0050 meters from the center of the cable. The plot of the
magnetic field strength B as a function of the e m f epsilon of the power supply is shown below.
The figure presents a scatterplot in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The horizontal axis is labeled “Epsilon, in volts,” and the numbers 0 through 10, in increments of 2, are indicated. The vertical axis is labeled “B, in times 10 to the negative 5 power teslas,” and the numbers 0 through 25, in increments of 5, are indicated. There are 4 data points in the scatter plot. The data are as follows. Note that all values are approximate.
Point 1 represents 2.5 volts and 2.5 times 10 to the negative 5 power teslas.
Point 2 represents 5 volts and 10.5 times 10 to the negative 5 power teslas.
Point 3 represents 7.6 volts and 14.7 times 10 to the negative 5 power teslas.
Point 4 represents 10 volts and 20 times 10 to the negative 5 power teslas.
(d)
1 Draw the best-fit line for the data.
2
Use the straight line to calculate the resistance R of the resistor used in the experiment.
(e) The lab group uses a multimeter to measure the resist ance of the resistor and determines that their
experimental value for R from part (d) is approximately 10% larger than that found with the multimeter.
Describe a physical reason that might account for this result.
(f) The lab group notices that when the current is reversed in the cable and the experiment is again performed,
the plot has a positive vertical axis intercept equal in magnitude to the negative vertical axis intercept in the
plot shown before part (d).
1 Describe a physical reason for the vertical axis intercept.
2
Describe a physical reason that the vertical axis intercept switches from negative to positive when the
current in the cable is reversed.
____ ____ ____
The figure presents a diagram of a capacitor that consists of a top and bottom capacitor plate. Both plates are horizontally oriented. The top capacitor plate has a charge density of positive sigma, and the bottom capacitor plate has a charge density of negative sigma. The plates are separated by a vertical distance d. Note that the figure is not drawn to scale.

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2018 Practice Exam
text:
A parallel plate capacitor is connected to a battery, fully charged, and then isolated from the battery. The plates
are given equal and opposite charge densities sigma , and the separation between the plates is d, as shown in the
figure above. The area of each plate has a value of A.
(a) Derive an expression for the energy stored in the capacitor. Express your answer in terms of sigma , d, A, and
physical constants, as appropriate.
The figure presents a diagram of a capacitor that consists of a top plate, a bottom capacitor plate, and a Metal Slab. All 3 components are horizontally oriented. The top capacitor plate has a charge density of positive sigma, and the bottom capacitor plate has a charge density of negative sigma. The plates are separated by a vertical distance d. The Metal Slab is positioned halfway between the two plates and has a vertical thickness x.
While the capacitor is isolated from the battery, an uncharged metal slab of thickness x and area Ais inserted
between the capacitor plates so that it is an equal distance from the two plates, as shown in the figure above.
(b) On the figure above, draw an arrow to indicate the direction of the electric field in the following
three regions of space between the capacitor plates. If the electric field is zero in any region, indicate
this by writing quote, E equals 0, end quotein that region.
1 The region between the top plate and the metal slab
2
The region inside the metal slab
3
The region between the metal slab and the bottom plate
(c) On the lines below, rank the regions according to the magnitude of the electric field, with 1 being the largest
magnitude. If any regions have the same magnitude of electric field, give them the same ranking.
The region between the top plate and the metal slab
The region inside the metal slab
The region between the metal slab and the bottom plate
____ ____ ____
Question 3 continues on the next page.
(d) Is the capacitance of the capacitor with the metal slab greater than, less than, or equal to the capacitance of
the original capacitor without the slab?
____ Greater than
____ Less than
____ Equal to
Justify your answer.
(e) When charged to the original charge density, is the energy stored in the capacitor with the metal slab greater
than, less than, or equal to the original energy stored in the capacitor calculated in part (a) ?
Greater than
Less than
Equal to
Justify your answer.
Some physics students conduct an experiment in which they charge the capacitor to the same potential
difference, isolate it from the battery, insert the metal slab, and measure the new potential difference across the
capacitor. This experiment is repeated with slabs of different thickness. The plot of the potential difference delta V
across the capacitor as a function of the thickness x of the slab is shown below. A best-fit line for the data has
been drawn.
The figure presents a scatterplot in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The horizontal axis is labeled “Slab Thickness x, in centimeters,” and the numbers 0 through 12, in increments of 2, are indicated. The vertical axis is labeled “Potential Difference delta V, in volts,” and the numbers 0 through 25, in increments of 5, are indicated. There are 5 data points in the scatter plot. The data are as follows. Note that all values are approximate.
Point 1 represents 2.3 centimeters, 19.2 volts.
Point 2 represents 3.3 centimeters, 18.6 volts.
Point 3 represents 5 centimeters, 12.5 volts.
Point 4 represents 7.3 centimeters, 8.2 volts.
Point 5 represents 8 centimeters, 5 volts.
A best fit line is also given. The line begins at the point representing 2 centimeters, 20 volts, and extends downward and to the right until it ends at the point representing 8. 8 centimeters, 4 volts.
(f) Use the best-fit line to calculate the following:
1 sigma , the charge density on the plates
2
d, the distance between the capacitor plates

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2019 Practice Exam
text:
Students design an experiment to determine the unknown dielectric constant
kappa of a plastic material.
A capacitor is created using two square aluminum plates of side length s equals 30 centimeters that are separated by
a distance d equals 1.0 millimeter . This capacitor is placed in a circuit with an ideal 6.0-volt battery, a resistor of resistance
R equals 500 ohms
, voltmeter V, and an open switch S, as shown above. A 1.0 millimeter thick piece of plastic is inserted
between the aluminum plates. The distance x that the plastic is inserted between the plates can be varied, and the
voltmeter is used to measure the potential difference V sub C across the capacitor. The switch is closed, and readings
from the voltmeter are recorded as a function of time t. The data are plotted to create the graph shown below.
The figure presents the graph of a curve in a plane with the origin labeled O. The horizontal axis is labeled t. Just to the right of the origin is a tick mark labeled t sub one half. The vertical axis is labeled V sub C, in volts, and the numbers 3 and 6 are indicated. The curve begins at the origin and moves steadily upward and to the right at first. It passes through the point with coordinates t sub one half, comma 3, and continues upward with a decreasing rate until it starts to level off as it approaches V sub C equals 6.
The time t sub one half shown above is the time for the capacitor to charge to half the potential difference of the battery.
(a) The potential difference across the capacitor as a function of time is modeled by the equation
V s
ub C equals V sub max, end subscript, times, open parenthesis, 1 minus e raised to the negative t over R C, power, close parenthesis
, where V sub max equals 6 volts
. Derive an expression for the capacitance C of the capacitor.
Express your answer in terms of t sub one half , R, and physical constants, as appropriate.
The data for x and t sub one half are recorded for several trials and
the value of C for each trial is calculated. The results
are shown in the chart below.
x, in meters
0.050 0.10 0.15 0.20 0.25 t s u b o
n e half, in microseconds
0.44 0.63 0.75 0.88 1.10
C, in nanofarads
1.27 1.82 2.16 2.54 3.17
(b) Plot the experimental value of the capacitance C as a function of the distance x on the graph below. Clearly
scale and label all axes, including units if appropriate. Draw a straight line that best represents the data.
The figure presents a 25 by 20 grid. The gridlines are dashed lines. There are bolder dashed lines drawn every 5 grid units in the horizontal and vertical directions.
(c) The capacitor in the lab can be treated as two capacitors in parallel, one with the dielectric and one with air
between the plates. Show that the capacitance can be expressed as
C equals, the fraction epsilon sub 0, end subscript, times S, and denominator d, end fraction, times, open parenthesis, s plus, x times open parenthesis, kappa minus 1, close parenthesis, close parenthesis.
.
Question 1 continues on the next page.
(d) Using the graph from part (b), calculate the value of the dielectric constant kappa .
(e) The students now want to verify the value for the permittivity constant, epsilon sub 0 . Using the graph from part (b),
calculate an experimental value for epsilon sub 0 .
(f) Assume the value found in part (e) is higher than the accepted value for the permittivity constant. State one
possible physical reason for this error and explain how it could have caused this error.

source: AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism 2019 Practice Exam
text:
A thin wire of length L has a uniform charge density
positive lambda . A cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius d is drawn
with the wire along its central axis, as shown above. Point P is located at the center of one end of the cylinder, a
distance d from the end of the wire. Point Q is on the edge of the cylinder directly above the center of the wire,
as shown above.
A student says, “Gauss’s law can be used to find the electric flux phi through the Gaussian surface.”
(a) Is the student’s statement correct or incorrect?
____ Correct
____ Incorrect
If you have chosen “Correct,” use Gauss’s law to find the electric flux phi through the Gaussian surface.
If you have chosen “Incorrect,” explain why the student’s reasoning is incorrect and why Gauss’s law cannot
be applied in this situation.
(b) Two students discuss whether or not they can use Gauss’s law to find the electric field at points P and Q.
At which of the points, if either, is Gauss’s law a useful method for finding the electric field?
_____ At point P only
____ At point Q only
_____ At both points P and Q
____ At neither point P nor point Q
Justify your answer.
(c) Assuming the electric potential is zero at infinity, show that the value for the electric potential at point P is
given by the following expression.
V
equals the fraction with numerator lambda and denominator 4 pi times epsilon sub 0, end fraction, times the natural log of, open parenthesis, the fraction L plus d, over d, close parenthesis
Figure 2 presents a diagram of wire located along a horizontal x axis. The wire begins on the left side of the axis at the origin, and is a distance of L along the axis. To the right of the wire is a point labeled P on the x axis. The distance from the right end of the wire to point P is d.
The wire is aligned along the x-axis with the origin at the left end of the wire, as shown in Figure 2 above.
(d) A positively charged particle of charge positive e and mass m is released from rest at point P. On the axes below,
sketch the kinetic energy K of the particle, the potential energy U of the wire-particle system, and the total
energy E sub total of the wire-particle system as functions of the particle’s position x. Clearly label each sketch
with K, U, and E sub total . Explicitly label any maximum with numerical values or algebraic expressions, as
appropriate.
Question 2 continues on the next page.
The figure presents the first quadrant of a coordinate plane, with the origin labeled O. The horizontal axis is labeled x. Just to the right of the origin on the x axis is a tick mark labeled L plus d.
Figure 3 presents a diagram of wire located along a horizontal x axis. The wire begins on the left side of the axis at the origin, and is a distance of L along the axis.
(e) Derive an expression for the magnitude of the electric field due to the wire as a function of the position
along the x-axis, where x is greater than L
. Express your answer in terms of x, L, lambda , and physical constants, as
appropriate.
The figure presents a circuit diagram consisting of a battery, a switch, 2 resistors, and an inductor. The left side of the circuit has a 12 volt battery. Moving up the left side and along the top of the circuit is an open switch labeled S. To the right of the open switch the circuit splits into to vertical branches. The first vertical branch has a resistor labeled R sub 1, 30 ohms. The second vertical branch has an inductor labeled L, 50 millihenries. The portion of the bottom/side, between the two vertical branches has a resistor labeled R sub 2 equals 20 ohms.

text:
The circuit shown above is constructed using an ideal
12 volts battery, an ideal switch S, and two resistors and an
inductor with the values shown. Switch S is closed. After a long time, the circuit reaches steady-state conditions.
(a) Calculate the current through R sub 1 .
(b) Calculate the current through the battery.
The switch is then opened at time t equals 0 .
(c) Determine the current in the inductor immediately after the switch is opened.
(d)
i. Determine the current in resistor R sub 1 immediately after the switch is opened.
ii. Which of the following statements is correct about the current through R sub 1 immediately after the switch
is opened?
_____ The current is up through R sub 1 .
_____ The current is down through R sub 1 .
_____ There is no current through R sub 1 .
Justify your answer.

potential?
____ Top end
____ Bottom end
Justify your answer.
Question 3 continues on the next page.

the switch is opened. Explicitly label the vertical axis intercept with a numerical value.
The figure presents the first quadrant of a coordinate plane, with the origin labeled O. The horizontal axis is labeled t, and the vertical axis is labeled V.
(g) Write but DO NOT solve a differential equation that could be solved for the current through the inductor as
a function of time after the switch is opened.
